🇸🇰 SK

ECB SAFE Survey — Wave 38 · Slovakia

Slovakia · Euro Area · Germany  |  Generated 07 Jul 2026

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Margin squeeze from both ends: Slovak labour costs rose for a net 84% of firms (up 21.78 pp) while turnover fell for a net 26% (down 11.60 pp) and profits dropped for a net 40% — a compression the euro area's near-flat turnover did not share.
  • Credit line liquidity stress: The Slovak credit line financing gap widened substantially from +4.8pp to +14.8pp, driven by financing need rising from +3.1pp to +12.3pp, even as the bank loan gap eased to -4.0pp — a cross-instrument divergence reflecting mounting short-term liquidity pressure.
  • Turnover outlook diverges: A net 7% of Slovak firms expected turnover to rise, down 14.15 pp from a net 21%, while euro area peers grew more optimistic at a net 26% — widening the SK–EA gap to nearly 19 pp.
  • Interest rate reversal: A net 5% of Slovak firms reported higher bank loan interest rates, a reversal of 13.8 pp from the prior quarter's net 8% easing, though still well below the EA's net 27% tightening.

    • Regulation overtakes skilled staff as Slovak firms' second most pressing problem

Financing Conditions

Slovak interest rate conditions reversed to tightening after prior quarter easing

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported higher interest rates on bank financing — a reversal of 13.8 pp from the prior quarter's net easing of 8%, though still well below the EA's net 27% and DE's net 38% tightening, implying increased debt servicing costs for borrowers.
  • Non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions) remained broadly stable, with a net 41% of Slovak firms (n=60) reporting tighter conditions — almost unchanged from the prior quarter's net 42% and approaching Germany's net 49%, putting pressure on firms' operational margins.
  • Credit availability eased marginally, with a net 6% of Slovak firms (n=62) reporting a reduction in the available size of loans or credit lines, compared with a net 7% in the previous quarter and the EA's net 9% — suggesting limited but persistent constraints on growth financing.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Credit line stress widened substantially, driven by surging Slovak firm need

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • The Slovak credit line gap widened substantially from +4.8pp to +14.8pp — exceeding the EA gap of +12.6pp by 2.2pp — driven primarily by a sharp rise in financing need from +3.1pp to +12.3pp, while availability deteriorated only marginally (from -1.7pp to -2.5pp), signalling tighter liquidity constraints for Slovak firms.
  • By contrast, the bank loan gap improved from -7.6pp to -4.0pp as need rose from -6.2pp to -2.6pp while availability held steady at +1.4pp, leaving Slovak firms notably less stressed than the EA (+1.7pp gap); this cross-instrument divergence — easing bank loans alongside tightening credit lines — is the defining feature of wave 38 financing conditions.
  • Trade credit conditions eased moderately, with the Slovak gap narrowing from +9.8pp to +6.3pp as need fell from +10.1pp to +7.1pp and availability improved marginally (from +0.3pp to +0.8pp), placing Slovakia 1.9pp less stressed than the EA (+8.2pp), suggesting some relief for firms reliant on supplier credit.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak firms' bank loan availability expectations deteriorated moderately in wave 38

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=130) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 38, moderating 3.2 pp from a near-neutral net 2% in the previous wave — converging with the EA, where expectations also worsened by 3.4 pp to a net 4%, signalling tighter financing conditions ahead.
  • Slovak firms were considerably more pessimistic about trade credit availability than EA peers, with a net 10% expecting deterioration (n=79) compared with a net 1% for the EA — a gap of 9.4 pp, suggesting persistent local supply tightness not yet visible in EA-wide data.
  • Expectations for credit line and bank overdraft availability edged further negative for Slovak firms (net 7%, n=138, easing marginally by 1.3 pp), remaining 3.2 pp more pessimistic than the EA's net 4% — indicating continued constraints on short-term liquidity options.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms prioritise working capital over investment amid hiring push

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • Inventory and working capital remained the dominant financing purpose for Slovak firms, cited by 55.7% (n=229) — 16.2 pp above the euro area's 39.5% and broadly unchanged from the prior quarter's 55.8%, suggesting liquidity pressures persist despite stable demand.
  • Hiring and training of employees rose to 31.1% of Slovak firms (n=227), up 4.2 pp from the previous quarter, contrasting with a 3.1 pp decline to 24.3% across the euro area, indicating a strategic focus on expansion despite tighter financing conditions.
  • Fixed investment financing fell 6.1 pp to 34.2% (n=229), leaving Slovak firms below the euro area average of 36.8%, having exceeded it in the prior quarter at 40.3% versus 39.4%, reflecting caution amid economic uncertainty.

Slovak bank willingness remained positive but eased marginally in wave 38

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 7.8% of Slovak firms (n=306) reported that banks' willingness to provide credit improved, easing marginally by 2.6 pp from a net 10.4% in the prior wave, yet remaining above the euro area's net 5.5% and well above Germany's net 4.4% deterioration — suggesting continued, though slightly weaker, support for Slovak firms' financing access.
  • Access to public financial support deteriorated notably for a net 33.8% of Slovak firms (n=298), worsening by 8.8 pp from a net 25.0% in wave 37, compared with a near-unchanged net 15.5% deterioration across the euro area — putting pressure on firms reliant on state-backed financing.
  • The general economic outlook deteriorated further for a net 44.4% of Slovak firms (n=442), declining by 6.1 pp from a net 38.3% in the prior wave and remaining 18.3 pp worse than the euro area's net 26.1% — indicating heightened macro headwinds for external financing conditions.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak turnover contraction deepened as labour costs outpaced euro area peers

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • Slovak labour costs rose for a net 84% of firms (n=229), up 21.78 pp from the prior wave's net 62%, widening the gap above the euro area's net 63% to over 20 pp — putting further pressure on margins.
  • A net 26% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported falling turnover, deteriorating 11.60 pp from the prior wave's net 15%, contrasting sharply with the euro area's near-flat net 1% — signalling a sharper demand downturn.
  • Slovak profits declined for a net 40% of firms (n=229), worsening 17.37 pp from the prior wave's net 22% — more than double the euro area's net 17% contraction pace.

Slovak turnover outlook fell sharply while euro area firms grew more optimistic

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 7% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to increase over the coming quarters, declining 14.15 pp from a net 21% in the previous wave — suggesting a significant erosion in confidence among Slovak firms — while euro area peers grew more optimistic, rising to a net 26% (n=5,266) from a net 23%, widening the SK–EA gap to nearly 19 pp.
  • In a notable contrast, Slovak firms' fixed investment expectations improved moderately to a net 15% (n=228, Δ +3.88 pp vs the prior wave), surpassing the euro area's net 9% (n=5,266), which itself eased by 3.13 pp from the prior wave — indicating Slovak firms are outpacing peers in capex despite weaker turnover expectations.
  • German firms' turnover expectations rose marginally to a net 19% (Δ +2.28 pp vs the prior wave), while fixed investment expectations slipped to a net −2% — underscoring that weak investment sentiment was concentrated outside Slovakia.

Slovak firms face persistent margin squeeze as input costs far outpace selling prices

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • Slovak firms anticipated non-labour input price rises of 8.31% (n=417), 2.47 pp above the EA's 5.84%, while selling price expectations of 4.27% (n=418) eased marginally by 0.36 pp from wave 37's 4.63%, leaving a near 4 pp wedge between costs and revenues — squeezing profit margins further.
  • Wage growth expectations for Slovak firms eased moderately to 4.21% (n=441), down 1.28 pp from wave 37's 5.49%, yet still 1.41 pp above the EA average of 2.80%, compounding margin compression alongside elevated input costs — eroding competitiveness.
  • Slovak firms' five-year inflation expectations rose to 8.57% (n=163), up 1.51 pp from wave 37's 7.06%, widening the gap to the EA's 6.28% and indicating that longer-run price pressures remain more entrenched in Slovakia than across the euro area — raising uncertainty for investment planning.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms report the highest upside five-year inflation risk balance

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 59% of Slovak firms (n=229) saw upside risks to five-year inflation expectations in wave 38, rising notably by 5.80 pp from a net 53% in the prior wave — the highest reading among the three regions, signalling heightened long-term pricing pressures for firms.
  • The euro area closed the gap moderately, with its net balance rising more sharply by 9.90 pp to a net 55% (n=5,278), compared with Slovak firms' smaller 5.80 pp increase, leaving Slovakia above the EA by 3.61 pp — implying Slovak firms remain more concerned about persistent inflation than their peers.
  • Germany's net upside inflation risk balance rose 9.67 pp to a net 51%, confirming a broad regional shift toward elevated inflation risk perceptions, though still below both SK and EA.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Regulation overtakes skilled staff as Slovak firms' second most pressing problem

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Regulation rose marginally to 6.18/10 (n=225), overtaking skilled staff availability (6.07/10, n=228) to become Slovakia's second most pressing problem among the seven tested — while the EA scored regulation lower at 5.89/10, highlighting a relatively higher regulatory burden on Slovak firms.
  • Costs of production or labour remained the most pressing problem among the seven tested for Slovak firms (n=228), scoring 6.19/10, though easing marginally by 0.15 points from the previous wave's 6.34/10, and remaining below the EA score of 6.48/10, suggesting some relief but still a dominant concern.
  • Availability of skilled staff or experienced managers eased notably by 0.50 points to 6.07/10 (n=228), the largest decline across all seven problems for Slovakia, yet still reflecting a moderate burden on a 1–10 scale — indicating persistent challenges despite recent improvements.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
